proposed_short: The bare gerund (no stare) used like an adverb or a reduced subordinate clause: manner, means, time, cause, condition. Its subject must match the main clause's.
proposed_long: The adverbial gerund is the bare -ando / -endo form used on its own, without stare, to modify the main verb or to compress a whole subordinate clause into one word. It covers manner and means (è entrato correndo, he came in running; sbagliando s'impara, one learns by making mistakes), time (tornando a casa, while coming home), cause (essendo tardi, since it was late), condition (volendo, if you want) and, with pur/pure in front, concession (pur essendo stanco, although tired). When its action precedes the main one it becomes the compound gerund (avendo finito, having finished). The governing rule is the SAME-SUBJECT constraint: the gerund's implicit subject must be the same as the main clause's, so 'vedendo Maria, l'ho salutata' means I saw her, not she saw me; when the two subjects differ, Italian keeps a full clause instead. It is a distinct construction from the progressive (stare + gerund), which frames an action as in progress rather than modifying another.
rationale: Used throughout the gerundio usage batch (the modal_means and temporal_causal leaves). The glossary has 'gerund' (the form), and this batch's sibling proposals add 'progressive' (stare + gerund) and 'compound gerund' (gerundio passato); 'adverbial gerund' is the fourth distinct sense, the bare gerund as adverbial subordinator, and is the whole subject of this usage branch. The same-subject constraint has no other glossary home.
